items.q: How do you say 'My sister is embarrassed' in Spanish?
items.correct: Mi hermana tiene vergüenza / está avergonzada.
items.why: Notorious mistake when discussing family/relationships. 'Embarazada' = PREGNANT, not embarrassed. To say embarrassed: tener vergüenza or estar avergonzado/a. Saying your sister is pregnant when you mean embarrassed causes laughter.

items.q: In Spain, how do you address a group of friends informally?
items.why: Students don't distinguish vosotros (informal plural, Spain) from ustedes (formal plural in Spain, but used for all plural in Latin America). In Spain: tú/vosotros = informal; usted/ustedes = formal. Latin America: only ustedes for plural.

items.q: Which Spanish 'you' do you use with a stranger or elderly person?
items.why: Students use tú with everyone like English 'you'. Spanish distinguishes FORMAL (usted) for politeness/distance and INFORMAL (tú) for friends/family. Usted takes third-person verb forms, which trips students up.

items.q: Which sentence correctly says 'I get on well with my sister'?
items.correct: Me llevo bien con mi hermana.
items.misconception: Me llevo bueno con mi hermana.
items.random_wrong: Yo llevo bien con mi hermana.
items.why: It's Me llevo bien con mi hermana.. “Me llevo bueno con mi hermana.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is the correct use of ser/estar: 'I am tired'?
items.why: It's Estoy cansado.. “Soy cansado.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many forms does the Spanish adjective 'simpático' have (masc sing, masc plur, fem sing, fem plur)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 forms. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
